question: NVIDIA reported record quarterly revenue of $68.1 billion, with data center revenue reaching $62.3 billion as AI infrastructure demand accelerated.
BYD's quarterly revenue declined 11.8% and earnings per share fell 57%, showing pressure from China's highly competitive EV market.
Alphabet's Google Cloud revenue grew 48%, but its planned $175 billion to $185 billion in 2026 capital spending raised concerns about AI investment returns.
Oracle and CoreWeave shares fell after reports of OpenAI growth concerns raised doubts about future AI infrastructure demand.
$NVDA just dropped another monster quarter - $68B revenue and data center still carrying the whole AI trade.
$AMZN sales are still growing, but a $200B AI capex plan and a double-digit after-hours drop make the spending story feel risky.
$TSLA finally showed higher profit again, but competition and heavy investment still make the stock hard to read.
$BYD revenue down and EPS crushed by more than half - China's EV price war looks painful.
We delivered record quarterly revenue, led by exceptional data center demand and continued adoption of our AI computing platform.
We achieved all-time records for company revenue and earnings per share, with iPhone and Services revenue reaching new highs.
Our cloud business continued to expand strongly, with Microsoft Cloud revenue growing 26% and remaining performance obligation rising sharply.
We increased annual net sales by 12%, while continuing to invest heavily in AI infrastructure to support long-term growth.
Apple reported that its greenhouse gas emissions remained more than 60% below 2015 levels even as the company grew.
Microsoft reported that its total Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions increased 23.4% from its 2020 baseline, partly due to AI and cloud expansion.
Google reduced data center energy emissions by 12% in 2024 and procured more than 8 GW of clean energy.
The Net-Zero Banking Alliance decided to cease operations after many member banks left the climate coalition.
U.S. real GDP growth slowed to a 0.5% annualized rate in Q4 2025 after growing 4.4% in Q3.
U.S. CPI rose 0.9% in March, with energy prices increasing 10.9% for the month.
China reached 5.0% GDP growth in 2025, meeting its target, but weak domestic consumption remained a longer-term risk.
India's real GDP is estimated to grow 7.6% in FY 2025-26, showing strong and sustained expansion.
JPMorgan Chase reported first-quarter 2026 net income of $16.5 billion and managed revenue of $50.5 billion, supported by strong firmwide performance.
Intel reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $13.6 billion, up 7% year over year, and issued a second-quarter revenue forecast above analyst expectations.
Nike projected a surprise current-quarter sales decline of 2% to 4%, while China sales fell 10% amid weak demand and stronger local competition.
Boeing's first-quarter 2026 revenue rose to $22.2 billion and backlog reached a record $695 billion, but the company still reported a loss and negative free cash flow.
$JPM pulling in $16.5B in quarterly profit while revenue keeps climbing shows the big banks are still in a strong position.
$INTC finally looks like it has real momentum again after beating Q1 expectations and guiding higher for next quarter.
$NKE's China problem is not going away, and a surprise sales-drop forecast makes the turnaround look shaky.
$BA backlog looks huge, but negative free cash flow makes it hard to call the recovery clean yet.
We reported strong first-quarter results, including $16.5 billion in net income, a 19% return on equity, and $50.5 billion in managed revenue.
First-quarter revenue increased 7% year over year, and we expect second-quarter revenue to remain above prior market expectations.
Revenue increased during the quarter, and total backlog reached a record level, although free cash flow remained negative.
Fourth-quarter earnings declined from the prior year as upstream earnings weakened and foreign currency effects reduced reported results.
Multilateral development banks provided a record $137 billion in climate finance in 2024, increasing support for climate-related projects.
Investors pressed Amazon, Microsoft, and Google for more detailed data-center water and power-use disclosures as AI infrastructure expanded.
Exxon sued California over climate disclosure rules that require large companies to report emissions and climate-related financial risks.
NatWest faced investor criticism after weakening some climate-related lending restrictions and removing several decarbonization targets.
The Federal Reserve kept interest rates unchanged at 3.5% to 3.75%, but the highly divided vote signaled uncertainty over inflation and future policy.
The U.S. goods trade deficit widened 5.3% in March as imports rose sharply, creating potential pressure on economic growth.
Euro zone economic confidence fell to its lowest level in three and a half years as energy-price pressure and geopolitical uncertainty weighed on sentiment.
U.S. core capital goods orders surged 3.3% in March, supported by AI-related investment and data-center construction.
Visa beat quarterly profit estimates as payments volume rose 9% and consumer spending remained resilient despite macro uncertainty.
SoFi posted record first-quarter results, but its shares fell after the company left its 2026 revenue forecast unchanged.
$GD jumping after stronger defense and aerospace results plus higher profit guidance makes the quarter look seriously strong.
$GEHC revenue grew, but cutting the annual profit outlook because chip, oil, and freight costs are rising is not a good look.

We achieved record revenue and loan-origination growth, but maintained our full-year guidance because macro uncertainty remains elevated.
Revised estimates showed that AI data centers in the U.K. could produce far more carbon emissions than officials had previously projected.
Support for U.S. environmental and social shareholder resolutions declined sharply amid political pressure and reduced investor backing.
Brazil created more formal jobs than expected in March, signaling stronger-than-anticipated labor market momentum.
Inflation rose in several major German states in April, pointing to renewed national price pressure from higher energy and raw material costs.
